evidence_quotes.quote: video games being made unavailable or entirely unplayable after purchase.

paragraphs.text: Dear European Commission, Thank you for the opportunity to provide feedback on the Digital Fairness Act. I would like to respectfully draw your attention to a growing issue in the digital entertainment sector: the increasing number of video games being made unavailable or entirely unplayable after purchase. This trend harms consumer rights and digital preservation and is a key concern of the Stop Destroying Video Games movement, which I fully support. A Widespread Problem Many consumers today purchase digital games at full price, expecting long-term access. Unfortunately, publishers can later delist, shut down, or disable these products, even when there are no legal, technical, or commercial justifications. This undermines trust and the idea of digital ownership. Real-World Examples: "The Crew" (Ubisoft, 2014) Launched at 59.99, millions bought this online racing game. In 2024, Ubisoft shut down its servers, making the game 100% unplayableeven in single-player mode. Buyers lost access to what they paid for. "Mirrors Edge Catalyst" (EA, 2016) Sold for full price, this single-player game was later delisted. While existing owners can still play it, no new users can legally access it. This harms preservation and consumer choice. Nintendos 3DS and Wii U eShop closures (2023) Entire libraries of games, including many EU-localized titles, became unavailable for new purchases. In some cases, users were also blocked from re-downloading games they owned. Sonys attempted PS3/Vita store shutdown (2021) After public backlash, Sony reversed its plan, but the attempt shows how fragile digital access remains. These cases are not exceptionstheyre signs of a broken model that gives publishers the power to revoke access to products people bought in good faith. What the Digital Fairness Act Should Address To ensure digital fairness, I urge the Commission to include the following principles in the final legislation: Preservation and Access Guarantees Digital games, especially those sold at full price, should remain accessible and playableparticularly single-player contentfor a minimum period (e.g., 10 years after purchase). Right to Repair and Emulation Consumers and preservation groups should be legally allowed to emulate or preserve games no longer supported by publishers, as part of broader cultural preservation efforts. Ban on Destructive Shutdowns Publishers should not be allowed to deliberately disable games after sale unless full refunds or offline alternatives are offered. Mandatory Sunset Notices Companies should be required to give at least 12 months notice before removing access to any digital game and offer users the ability to download or archive their purchase. Recognition of Games as Cultural Works The EU already protects film and music as cultural heritage. Games deserve the same recognition and preservation protections, especially when created or published in the EU. Final Thoughts The Stop Destroying Video Games movement is not anti-industry. It supports innovation and online services, but also defends consumer rights, digital preservation, and cultural access. The Digital Fairness Act is a chance for the EU to lead in protecting digital ownership and ensuring that sold products remain usable. Thank you for considering this feedback and for working toward a fairer digital future for all EU citizens. Sincerely
